segments.s026-g49: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing reported speech. چون here has a temporal/conditional sense 'when/as' (not 'because'). بنده is a noun 'servant' (self‑designation), and در پیش together form a locative phrase 'in the presence of' (در = in; پیش = presence/of), followed by فرزند تو 'your son' (noun + possessive), so the whole chunk means 'that, when (I am) a servant in the presence of your son.'
segments.s026-g50: بباشم is 1st person singular present-subjunctive/optative of 'to be' (an expression of state or wish after the subordinate clause 'که چون'), meaning 'I be / I may be / I shall be.' پرستنده is a noun 'worshipper/devotee' functioning as a predicative label, and پند تو is a genitive phrase 'your counsel/advice' (پند = advice; تو = your). Together: 'I shall be a worshipper of your counsel.'
segments.s026-g51: سه سالش is 'three years' with the enclitic -ش 'its/his' attached to سال (literally 'three years of it/him'); همی is the archaic/poetic imperfective particle meaning 'regularly/still' (marks habitual or continuous action), and داد is the past verb 'gave'. زان is the prepositional/relative 'from that' or 'of that', followed by گاو شیر 'cow milk' where شیر means 'milk'. The phrase thus says he 'gave milk of that cow for three years.'
segments.s026-g52: هشیوار and بیدار are adjectival/epithetic words meaning 'alert' and 'awake' (often paired in poetry). زنهارگیر is a compound agent-noun 'warner' or 'one who cries beware' used here as an epithet. All three stand in apposition (poetic epithets) describing the cow or the giver, rendered together as 'the alert, wakeful warner.'
segments.s026-g9: This is an elliptical, adjectival phrase: 'جهان را' (with the object marker را marking 'the world' as definite) is compared with 'چو باران' ('like rain'). The phrase 'به بایستگی' expresses manner — 'in a fitting/necessary way' — where به + noun functions adverbially ('in/by ...-ness'). No explicit finite verb appears here; the predicate is understood from the larger clause.
segments.s026-g10: Parallel to the previous half-line: 'روان را' (the soul, marked definite with را) is likened 'چو دانش' ('as/like knowledge'). 'به شایستگی' again uses به + noun to indicate manner/degree — 'in due worthiness/merit'. The construction is elliptical (verb implied from context) and balanced with the first segment.
segments.s026-g11: 'بسر بر' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'as/while (time) passed' with 'بسر' (to pass) followed by the preposition بر. 'همی' is the durative particle indicating continued action; 'گشت' is the verb 'turned/was turning'. 'گردان سپهر' is a postnominal descriptive phrase 'the revolving heavens' (گردان modifying سپهر). Word order is poetic: temporal/adverbial phrase + continuous particle + verb + noun phrase.
segments.s026-g12: 'شده رام' is a compound predicate: شده (become) + رام (tame)—'became tamed'. 'با آفریدون' is a prepositional phrase 'with/for Afrīdūn' (here meaning 'toward Afrīdūn' or 'in relation to Afrīdūn'). 'به مهر' indicates the cause or means: 'by/through مهر (affection/love)'; به functions adverbially ('by/through'). The clause states the heavens were tamed in relation to Afrīdūn by love.
segments.s026-g13: The noun phrase 'همان گاو کش' ('that cow-slayer' — گاو + agentive کش) is the subject. 'نام برمایه بود' is a nominal predicate: نام ('name' or reputation) followed by برمایه ('capital/source of wealth') and the copula بود ('was'). In archaic/poetic usage 'نام' can indicate the title or how he was known; the clause links his name/title with being a source of capital.
segments.s026-g14: 'ز گاوان' ('from the cattle') is an ablative/source phrase (ز = 'from'). 'ورا' ('to him/for him') indicates possession/benefit. 'برترین پایه بود' is the predicate: 'was the foremost/most excellent foundation' — i.e., his chief support or greatest wealth came from his cattle. Word order (prepositional phrase + dative + predicate) is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s026-g15: 'ز مادر جدا شد' is a simple verb phrase: 'he parted/was separated from his mother' (ز = from, جدا = apart, شد = became/happened). 'چو طاووس نر' is a simile ('like a male peacock') modifying the manner of separation. Word order places the ablative phrase first for emphasis, then the verb, then the simile.
segments.s026-g16: This is an elliptic descriptive clause linked to the previous verb: 'بهر موی' ('for/to the hair/plumage') uses بهر = 'for/toward'. 'بر' = 'on' and 'تازه رنگی دگر' = 'a fresh/ new different color' (تازه 'new', رنگی 'being colored/colouring', دگر 'other/different'). The finite verb ('شد' or equivalent) is omitted but understood from the prior clause — the hair/plumage took on a new color.
segments.s026-g33: فرانک is the grammatical subject (a proper name). بد + ش is a sequence where بد is an epithet/adjective (here ‘bad’ or an epithet used as a name) and ش is the 3rd-person possessive clitic (‘his’), so the phrase literally reads “his bad (name)”; نام is the predicate noun ‘name’ placed after that phrase. The copula بود (past ‘was’) follows and applies to both predicates joined by و (and), so the whole clause means “[His] name was Bad, and (he) was auspicious.”
segments.s026-g34: به مهر فریدون is a prepositional phrase marking the object/target of affection (‘toward/for Fereydun, in love’). دل is the subject ‘heart’ and آگنده is a predicate adjective ‘filled, full’; بود is the past copula ‘was’. Word order is typical of classical/poetic Persian: prepositional phrase + subject + predicate + copula.
segments.s026-g35: پر از is an idiomatic construction meaning ‘full of’ (پر governs از + noun). داغ دل is a genitive/compound: داغ ‘burning grief’ qualified by دل ‘heart’ (i.e. ‘the heart’s wound/grief’). خسته modifies دل (دل خسته = ‘weary heart’), and روزگار after it gives the sense of the burdens of ‘time/fate’ — poetic noun order places modifiers around the head noun.
segments.s026-g36: همی is an adverb of continued action (‘still/kept’). رفت is the past tense verb ‘went’. پویان is a present-participial adjective ‘seeking’ used as an epithet modifying the subject (i.e. ‘going, seeking’). بدان is an older compound sense of به + آن ‘to that’, so the phrase means ‘went to that (place)’. مرغزار is the destination ‘meadow’. The clause thus reads ‘he kept going, seeking, to that meadow.’
segments.s026-g37: کجا is an interrogative/adversative particle here meaning roughly ‘where / was there (ever)’. نامور modifies گاو (‘a renowned cow’). برمایه is a postpositive adjective/attribute meaning ‘of capital/value’ (poetic word order often places such modifiers after the noun). بود is the past copula ‘was’. The whole gives a rhetorical question about existence of such a valuable cow.
segments.s026-g38: که is the relative particle ‘that’ introducing a clause qualifying the previous noun. بایسته is a predicate adjective ‘fitting, proper’. بر تنش is a prepositional phrase: بر ‘on/upon’ + تن ‘body’ + ش (3rd-person possessive clitic) = ‘on its body’. پیرایه is the noun ‘ornament’, and بود is the past copula. Grammatically: ‘that a fitting ornament was upon its body.’
segments.s026-g39: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘before, in the presence of’. نگهبان is the noun ‘guardian’ and آن مرغزار is a genitive-like specification ‘of that meadow’ (the guardian of that meadow). The whole prepositional phrase sets the scene ‘before the guardian of that meadow.’
segments.s026-g40: خروشید is a past intransitive verb ‘it roared/bellowed’. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. بارید is another past verb ‘fell/rained’ used metaphorically; خون is the noun ‘blood’ (subject of بارید in this figurative expression). بر کنار is the prepositional phrase ‘upon the edge/bank’. The clause uses vivid, personified imagery: ‘it roared and blood rained on the bank.’
segments.s026-g17: شده is the perfect/passive-like verb ‘has been / was made’ placed before the noun (classical inversion). انجمن is the noun ‘assembly’; بر سرش is a locative phrase ‘upon his head’ where سر takes the enclitic ش = ‘his’. بخردان is a plural agent noun ‘the wise ones’ (in apposition to انجمن), so the clause reads literally ‘an assembly of wise men was made upon his head.’
segments.s026-g18: This phrase lists who the ‘wise ones’ are: ستاره‌شناسان ‘astrologers/stargazers’ and هم موبدان ‘and also the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)’. هم adds ‘also/too’; both nouns are in apposition to بخردان and complete the description of the assembly.
segments.s026-g19: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause here, meaning ‘that/for which’; کس is understood here in the negative sense ‘no one’ (negation is realized by the نه that follows in the next segment); در جهان is the locative ‘in the world’; گاو چونان = ‘a cow/bull like that’; ندید is simple past ‘saw’ (so literally ‘that no one in the world saw such a cow’). Note classical word order and that the explicit negator appears in the next clause but applies to this verb as well.
segments.s026-g20: نه is the negative particle ‘not/nor’. از introduces the source/agent ‘from’; پیرسر کاردانان is a descriptive compound meaning ‘the gray‑headed (پیرسر) skilled/knowledgeable ones (کاردانان)’ i.e. ‘the old experienced men’; شنید is past tense ‘heard’ or ‘had heard of’. Together: ‘nor did the aged experts hear of (such a thing).’
segments.s026-g21: This is inverted classical order: زمین کرده (کرده = ‘made/filled’) takes زمین as its object, so the clause means ‘[Zahhak] made the earth full…’. ضحاک is the subject following the verb (common in older Persian). پر گفت و گوی = ‘full of speech/talk’ where پر = ‘full (of)’ and گفت و گوی is a binomial noun ‘speech/conversation’ (i.e. clamor, talk).
segments.s026-g22: به گرد جهان = ‘around/about the world’ (به + گرد + جهان). هم adds ‘also/likewise’. بدین is here composed of ب- (prefix) + دین (in older usage functioning as ‘this’/‘with this’ in the phrase), so بدین جست و جوی = ‘with this search / in this quest’. جست (here) means ‘seek/search’ and جوی is the noun ‘search’ (جست و جوی forms a verbal-nominal collocation ‘sought/searching’). Overall: ‘and thus he searched round the world.’
segments.s026-g23: فریدون is the proper name; که introduces a relative clause ‘who/which’; بودش is the past of بودن ‘to be’ with the enclitic ش attached (classical form), here meaning ‘was’ (so ‘who was’); پدر آبتین = ‘the father of Abtin’ (پدر + name in genitive). Together: ‘Fereydun, who was Abtin’s father’.
segments.s026-g24: شده is the perfect/passive-like ‘had become’; تنگ is a predicate adjective ‘narrow/tight’. بر آبتین is a dative/experiencer phrase ‘for Abtin’ (بر + name = ‘to/for someone’ in classical Persian); بر زمین is the locative ‘on the earth’. Together the clause means ‘the earth had grown narrow for Abtin’ (i.e. Abtin felt confined on earth).
segments.s026-g1: برآمد is the past (perfect) verb 'arose' or 'appeared'. برین is a compound: بر (a prefix/element meaning 'high' or 'on') + the suffix -ین forming an adjective 'lofty/high' (a poetic form); روزگار is the noun 'time/age' and دراز is an adjective 'long'. Word order is typical poetic Persian (verb/noun/adjective cluster after the verb), so literally 'arose the lofty long age' = 'a long, lofty age arose.'
segments.s026-g2: کشید is the past of کشیدن and here functions as 'stretched' or 'drew out' (poetic usage). اژدهافش is an adjectival/epithetic compound 'dragon‑like' (literally 'dragon‑breathing/ -spreading') describing the extended thing; به + تنگی فراز is an adverbial phrase 'into/to the narrow high place' (به + noun phrase). The clause describes a motion/extension upward: 'it stretched, dragon‑like, toward the narrow heights.'
segments.s026-g3: خجسته is an epithet 'auspicious/blessed' modifying the proper name فریدون. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (classical Persian), مادر is 'mother', and بزاد is an old past form meaning 'was born' (passive/ intransitive birth). The sequence is straightforward: 'auspicious Fereydun was born of (his) mother.'
segments.s026-g4: جهان را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker ('the world' as the domain), یکی دیگر is 'one other' i.e. 'another', آمد is the simple past 'came', and نهاد here is the past of نهادن 'placed' (used semi‑statically to mean 'was put/brought into existence'). Together the phrase means 'another was placed/brought into the world' (an impersonal or passive sense).
segments.s026-g5: ببالید is an intransitive past meaning 'grew' or 'flourished'. برسان here functions idiomatically as 'reaching to / up to' (comparative result) and سرو سهی is a poetic compound 'a stately (dark/tall) cypress' used as an image of height and beauty. So the clause says 'he grew until he reached the stature of a stately cypress.'
segments.s026-g6: همی is an older continuous/emphatic particle 'continually/ever', تافت is past 'shone' (from تافتن). ز here is the poetic 'from', and the following sequence (و + فر شاهنشهی) stands for 'from him the royal/imperial glory' — note that in classical orthography 'زو' = ز + او (from him). The whole reads 'from him imperial splendor continually shone.'
segments.s026-g7: جهانجوی is a descriptive compound 'world‑seeking' (an ambitious or empire‑seeking figure) used as a substantive; با + فر جمشید بود is 'had/was with the glory of Jamshid' — با marks accompaniment/possession and بود is past 'was'. Together: 'the world‑seeker had Jamshid's (royal) glory.'
segments.s026-g8: به کردار is an adverbial phrase 'in manner/behavior' (به functioning to form an adverbial), تابنده خورشید is a descriptive phrase 'the shining sun' (تابنده qualifying خورشید), and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause means 'in his conduct he was shining/resplendent like the sun.'
segments.s026-g25: This is a coordinated descriptive phrase: 'گریزان و' (an adjective/participle + conjunction) links to the following predicate. 'از خویشتن گشته' uses the perfect participle 'گشته' (become) with the ablative preposition 'از' + emphatic reflexive 'خویشتن' to mean 'become of/with himself.' The clause ends with the adjective/noun 'سیر' (sated/weary), so the whole segment means 'fleeing, and become sated/tired of himself.'
segments.s026-g26: Main verb phrase in past tense: 'برآویخت' is a past (perfect/simple past) intransitive verb meaning 'sprang up' or 'leapt upon.' 'ناگاه' is an adverb meaning 'suddenly' modifying the verb. The prepositional phrase 'بر کام شیر' uses 'بر' + noun to indicate direction/target ('onto/into'), with 'کام' in the sense 'appetite/maw' and 'شیر' 'lion' — an idiom for 'into the lion's jaws.'
segments.s026-g27: 'از آن روزبانان ناپاک مرد' uses the preposition 'از' to mark partitive/identification: 'of/those among the heralds.' 'آن' specifies 'those,' 'روزبانان' is the plural 'heralds/announcers,' and 'ناپاک' postmodifies (or qualifies) them as 'unclean/immoral.' The final noun 'مرد' here functions as 'a man' or 'one person' from that group: 'an unclean man of those heralds.'
segments.s026-g28: 'تنی چند' is an idiomatic quantifier meaning 'a few' or 'some (people)' (literally 'a few bodies'). 'روزی' is temporal ('one day' / 'on a day') acting as an adverbial. 'بدو' is the clitic/phrase 'to him' (indirect object). 'باز خورد' is a compound where 'خورد' functions idiomatically as 'befell/happened' and 'باز' here reinforces the action (often glossed 'happened to him'); taken together the phrase means 'one day a few (men) came upon him' or 'one day it happened to him that a few (men) [came].'
segments.s026-g29: 'گرفتند و بردند' are two coordinated past-tense verbs in the 3rd-person plural: 'they seized and they carried/took.' 'بسته' is a past participle used predicatively/adjectivally meaning 'bound/tied.' 'چو یوز' is a simile introduced by 'چو' meaning 'like/as' and 'یوز' ('cheetah' / hunting animal) — together 'bound like a cheetah.' The participle follows the verbs to describe the state in which he was taken.
segments.s026-g30: This clause gives the destination and time: 'بر سر آورد' is an idiomatic compound verb meaning 'to bring before/present (to someone's head/presence)' — here rendered 'brought before.' 'ضحاک' is the proper name, the indirect/addressee of the bringing. 'روز' at the end is the temporal adverb 'that day.' Word order is typical of epic Persian: verb phrase containing 'آورد' (bring) comes before the addressee and time adjunct.
segments.s026-g31: Noun phrase + temporal clause: 'خردمند مام فریدون' places the adjective 'خردمند' (wise) before the noun 'مام' (here 'nurse' or wet‑nurse) with the possessor 'فریدون' following, so 'Fereydun's wise nurse.' 'چو' is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when,' and 'دید' is the past-tense verb 'saw' — 'when Fereydun's wise nurse saw...'.
segments.s026-g32: 'که' introduces the subordinate content clause 'that...'. The preposition 'بر' marks the affected party 'جفت او' ('his mate/companion'); the repeated 'بر' before 'چنان' is a poetic doubling to emphasize the direction/result. 'چنان بد' = 'such bad/evil,' and 'رسید' is the past-tense verb 'reached/befell.' Together: 'that upon his companion such evil befell.'
segments.s026-g41: بدو گفت is the classical word order 'to him said' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' plus گفت = 'said'), introducing reported speech with the conjunction ک (here in the fused form کاین = 'that/this'). کاین کودک شیرخوار is a demonstrative phrase 'this/that infant (suckling)', with شیرخوار as an attributive noun specifying the kind of کودک.
segments.s026-g42: ز من روزگاری is an adverbial/possessive phrase 'of me / belonging to me for some time' (ز = 'from/of', من = 'me', روزگاری = 'for a time'). بزنهار دار is an archaic idiom: بزنهار (an old warning/exhortation) plus دار (here functioning as an imperative/verb 'hold/keep') = 'keep (him) with care/guard for the daytime/for a while'.
segments.s026-g43: پدروارش is built from پدروار ('fatherly') plus the possessive suffix -ش ('his'): an imperative/read‑as‑advice formula 'be his father'. از مادر اندر پذیر is 'receive (him) from the mother' with از + مادر = 'from the mother' and اندر پذیر = 'take/accept in' (پذیر here = 'take/receive'). The whole clause is an instruction with the subject (you) omitted.
segments.s026-g44: وزین is the fused classical connective 'و زین' (= 'and/from this/and this'), linking to what follows. گاو نغزش is 'the cow (گاو) + adjective نغز (naghz = 'fine, delightful') with a possessive suffix -ش implied ('his/its'), so 'this fine cow of his/for him'. بپرور is an imperative/jussive verb 'nurture/bring up' (ب‑ prefix on the verb پرور), and به شیر marks the means 'on/with milk' (شیر = 'milk' here).
segments.s026-g45: وگر باره خواهی is a conditional 'and if (at any) time you desire' (وگر = 'if', باره = 'occasion/again', خواهی = 2nd‑person present 'you wish'). روانم تراست = روان + -م ('my soul') + تراست (compound copula = 'is yours' where ترا = 'to/for you' + ست = 'is'): 'my soul is yours'.
segments.s026-g46: گروگان کنم جان is a straightforward clause 'I will make (کنم) my life (جان) a hostage (گروگان)' — کنم is the present/jussive form used to express willingness or future action. بدان کت هواست is an elliptic verbless clause linking purpose/result: بدان (literally 'to/for that') + هواست (هوا = 'desire/longing' + ست = 'is') = 'for that is (my) desire' (کت here is an archaic/formal particle or connective in the text; its precise lexical role is idiomatic).
segments.s026-g47: پرستنده بیشه و گاو نغز is a noun phrase 'the admirer/devotee (پرستنده) of the grove (بیشه) and of the fine cow (گاو نغز)'. Classical Persian places the adjective نغز after the noun (گاو نغز = 'the delightful/fine cow'); the whole phrase functions as the subject of the next clause.
segments.s026-g48: چنین داد پاسخ means 'thus gave an answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = past 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). بدان is a short linking/locative particle 'to/at that' (here 'to that pure‑minded one' or 'in response to him'), and پاک مغز is an epithet 'pure‑minded' (پاک = 'pure', مغز = 'mind/core'), describing the addressee of the reply.

sentences.s026-s25: That when I am a servant in the presence of your son, I will be an admirer of your counsel.
sentences.s026-s26: For three years he gave the milk of that cow — the alert, wakeful warner.
sentences.s026-s5: He set the world, like rain, in fitting order, and the soul, like knowledge, in fitting worthiness.
sentences.s026-s6: As time passed, the revolving heavens turned; (they) became tamed to Afrīdūn through affection.
sentences.s026-s7: That cow-slayer was a name and source of capital; from the cattle he had the highest foundation (i.e., his chief wealth came from cattle).
sentences.s026-s8: He separated from his mother like a male peacock; his plumage assumed a fresh, different color.
sentences.s026-s17: The Frank was called Bad and was auspicious; his heart was filled with love for Fereydun.
sentences.s026-s18: Full of the heart’s burning anguish and wearied by the times, he kept going, seeking, to that meadow.
sentences.s026-s19: Where was there a famed cow of value, that a fitting ornament should be on its body?
sentences.s026-s20: It roared before the guardian of that meadow, and blood rained upon the bank.
sentences.s026-s9: An assembly of sages — astrologers and even the mobeds — had been gathered over his head.
sentences.s026-s10: for no one in the world had seen a cow like it, nor had the gray‑haired experts heard of one.
sentences.s026-s11: Zahhak filled the earth with talk, and likewise went about the world in this search.
sentences.s026-s12: Fereydun — who was Abtin’s father — found that the earth had grown narrow for Abtin.
sentences.s026-s1: A long, lofty age arose; its dragon-like length stretched up into the narrow heights.
sentences.s026-s2: The auspicious Fereydun was born of his mother; another one was brought into the world.
sentences.s026-s3: He grew to the stature of a stately cypress; from him the imperial radiance continually shone.
sentences.s026-s4: The world‑seeker possessed Jamshid's royal glory; in conduct he was resplendent like the shining sun.
sentences.s026-s13: Fleeing, and grown weary of himself, he suddenly leapt into the lion's maw.
sentences.s026-s14: One day a few of those unclean heralds fell in with him.
sentences.s026-s15: They seized him and carried him off, bound like a cheetah, and brought him before Zahhak that day.
sentences.s026-s16: When Fereydun's wise nurse saw that such evil had befallen his companion.
sentences.s026-s21: He said to him, "This suckling child is of mine — keep him for a while by day."
sentences.s026-s22: Be his foster‑father; take him from his mother, and from this fine cow nourish him with milk.
sentences.s026-s23: And if you ever wish it, my soul is yours; I will give my life as a hostage — for that is my desire.
sentences.s026-s24: The devotee of the grove and of the fine cow thus gave that answer to the pure‑minded one.
